title: The Lord, God of Heaven and Earth
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: The Lord, God of Heaven and Earth - Elder Scott Hollander - Isaiah 66.txt
outline: Heaven is my throne and earth my footstool — what house can you build Me? (v.1)
The one I esteem: humble, contrite, trembling at My Word (v.2)
The birth of a nation in a day — the restored people of God (vv.7-8)
Final vision: new heavens, new earth, and ongoing worship (vv.22-23)
summary: An AM sermon on Isaiah 66, the grand closing chapter of Isaiah, delivered by Elder Scott Hollander. The transcendent God—whose throne is heaven and footstool is earth—looks not for grand buildings but for humble, contrite hearts that tremble at His Word. The chapter moves from judgment on false worship to the miraculous rebirth of God's people, and concludes with the glorious vision of the new creation where all flesh comes to worship.
transcript:
Isaiah 66 is both the concluding chapter of Isaiah and its climactic declaration of coming judgment for the wicked and blessing for God's children in the new heaven and the new earth. If Isaiah 66 were summed up in a single sentence, the commentator Paul Kim captured the essence well: *Yahweh comforts the humble, but judges the wicked.* Interestingly, Isaiah 66 contains both poetry and prose. Though it mixes literary devices, the chapter is generally structured as a chiasm — with the theme of Yahweh's judgment against his enemies at the beginning (vv. 1–6) and at the end (vv. 15–24), and the theme of Yahweh's blessings to his people centered in the middle.

Isaiah 66 also serves as an echo and expansion of Isaiah 65. The phrases of Isaiah 65:12 are repeated nearly verbatim in Isaiah 66:4, with the primary change being a shift in pronoun from the second-person "you" to the third-person "they."

> *"Because I called but you did not answer, I spoke but you did not listen — but you did evil in my eyes, and that in which I do not delight you chose."* (Isaiah 65:12)

The actions of the wicked in their corrupt worship described in Isaiah 65 are expanded in Isaiah 66. The promise of the remnant from Isaiah 65 is repeated in Isaiah 66:2, as is the nourishment and delight of the faithful contrasted with the treatment of the wicked (Isaiah 66:10–14). And the picture of the new heavens and new earth described in Isaiah 65:17–25 is concluded in Isaiah 66:18–24.

## Yahweh Is Enthroned in Heaven (v. 1)

The opening verse has the Lord speaking about heaven and simultaneously referencing the temple in Jerusalem. It is significant that Isaiah concludes this great book with a focus on the temple on the holy hill of Jerusalem — the temple and Zion have been central elements throughout Isaiah. Yet in this passage, when he references the temple, he turns the notion on its head. Though the temple and the sacrificial system were an important aspect of the worship protocol the Lord had established, God makes clear it is not rote motion and legalism he desires. It is man's heart that Yahweh is after. What appears to be a statement of disregard for the temple is simply the Lord stressing that the heart of worship is more important than the location and mechanics of worship.

This morning we are studying just the first two verses of Isaiah 66, reflecting on Yahweh's description of himself. These two verses place the Lord enthroned in heaven as the sovereign creator of heaven and earth. No man-made structure is capable of upholding the glory of the God of the universe. The Lord is the owner of heaven and earth because he is its creator — and as creator and owner, he alone dictates what he requires of mankind: a humble spirit and the fear of his word.

> *"Thus says the Lord: Heaven is my throne and earth is my footstool. Where is the house that you will build me? And where is the place of my rest?"* (Isaiah 66:1)

People contemplating God's habitation often picture him in a spatially mystical heaven. Isaiah 63:15 refers to heaven as the lofty dwelling place of God's holiness and glory. In 2 Corinthians 12:2, Paul describes being caught up to the third heaven. Biblical scholars distinguish three heavens: first, the sky above the earth; second, what we call outer space; and third, what Paul described as the throne room of heaven, the sacred place where the full majesty of the Lord is on full display.

However, to constrain God even to that spatial location is impossible. In Jeremiah 23:24, Yahweh declares: *"Do I not fill heaven and earth?"* John Calvin puts it this way: *"By assigning heaven for his habitation, he means that the majesty of God fills all things and is everywhere diffused, and that he is so far from being shut up in the temple that he is not shut up or confined with any place whatever. The Scripture often teaches that God is in heaven, not that he is shut up in it, but in order that we may raise our minds above the world and may not entertain any low or carnal or earthly conceptions of him."*

As children declare when they recite their first catechism: God is a Spirit and does not have a body like men, and God is everywhere.

The Lord declares in Isaiah 66:1 that the heavens are his throne. The great expanse of heaven is insignificant to the greatness of Yahweh. Heaven is merely the place from which God has determined to figuratively sit and reign in power and majesty over his creation. Mankind's massive expanse of earth is merely a footstool in the dominion of Adonai Elohim, the sovereign God.

Though David desired to build a temple for the Lord — a house of rest for the Ark of the Covenant and the footstool of God — Solomon acknowledged the limitations of such an endeavor at the temple's dedication:

> *"But will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house that I have built!"* (1 Kings 8:27)

In Acts 7, Stephen quotes Isaiah 66:1–2 in his sermon before his martyrdom:

> *"However, the Most High does not dwell in temples made with hands, as the prophet says: 'Heaven is my throne and earth is my footstool. What house will you build for me, says the Lord, or what is the place of my rest? Has not my hand made all these things?'"* (Acts 7:48–50)

Note a subtle difference: where Isaiah asks *where* a house might be built, Stephen's quotation asks *what kind of* house could be built. Despite that minor variation, both convey the same truth: nothing man-made — from wherever it is built or of whatever it is made — is sufficient to house the King of the universe.

Luke tells us in Acts 7:58 that the young man Saul was present during this speech, collecting garments as they prepared to stone Stephen. As a scholar of Scripture in his own right, one wonders if Stephen's words were echoing in Paul's ears when he later declared to the Athenians: *"The God who made the world and everything in it, the Lord of heaven and earth, does not live in temples made by man"* (Acts 17:24).

When Isaiah was brought to the throne room of heaven in Isaiah 6, he described that the whole temple could barely contain merely the edges of the Lord's kingly robe:

> *"I saw the Lord sitting upon a high and exalted throne, and the edge of his robe filling the temple."* (Isaiah 6:1)

Heaven represents the place of ultimate power and authority, and it is there that the one true God has established his throne. This was so significant that Jesus warned against swearing by heaven or earth, because they are God's places of authority — not playthings for people to toss around at their whim:

> *"But I say to you, do not take an oath at all, either by heaven, for it is the throne of God, or by the earth, for it is his footstool, or by Jerusalem, for it is the city of the Great King."* (Matthew 5:34–35)

David likewise confirms in Psalm 103:19: *"The Lord has established his throne in the heavens, and his kingdom rules over all."*

It is from this throne room in heaven — as we saw in Psalm 2 — that Yahweh laughs at human rulers who believe they can thwart his sovereignty. Yet even as Yahweh's position in heaven is a threat to his enemies, it is a source of comfort for his people. Nehemiah, in his prayer (Nehemiah 1:4–6), continued fasting and praying before the God of heaven:

> *"O Lord God of heaven, the great and awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments — let your ear be attentive and your eyes open to hear the prayer of your servant that I now pray before you day and night."*

This is consistent with the teaching of Jesus in Matthew 6, who taught us to pray: *"Our Father in heaven..."* Our Father in heaven is the sovereign of heaven and earth — enthroned above all creation and able to see and respond to the pleas of his people. There is no one above our Father Yahweh to whom we may plead for mercy or forgiveness.

In this realization, believers of every age should echo the prayer of David:

> *"Blessed are you, O Lord, the God of Israel our Father, forever and ever. Yours, O Lord, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the majesty, for all that is in the heavens and in the earth is yours. Yours is the kingdom, O Lord, and you are exalted as head above all. Both riches and honor come from you, and you rule over all. In your hand are power and might, and in your hand it is to make great and to give strength to all. And now we thank you, our God, and praise your glorious name."* (1 Chronicles 29:10–13)

## Yahweh Is Creator of Heaven and Earth (v. 2)

> *"For all these things my hand has made, and so all these things came to be, declares the Lord."* (Isaiah 66:2a)

Yahweh is the sovereign God — Adonai Elohim — of heaven and earth because he is the creator of heaven and earth. As verse 1 declared that no suitable place on earth can serve as the habitation of the Lord, the implication of verse 2 is that since he created everything that exists, how could any creature establish an adequate sanctuary? Where the Hebrew Old Testament states that all things came into existence because God created them, the Septuagint renders this passage with the statement that since God created everything, they are his possession. Can man take something that already belongs to God and somehow fashion it into something more suitable for God's glory?

It is the Creator's prerogative to make heaven his throne and earth his footstool, because he made them for his own glory. In 2 Kings 19:15, King Hezekiah acknowledged this implication in prayer:

> *"O Lord, the God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim, you are the God, you alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth; you have made heaven and earth."*

Hezekiah acknowledged Yahweh's rightful place of authority because he is the creator of heaven and earth, and it is his prerogative to direct the affairs of man. As Proverbs asserts: *"The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the Lord; he turns it wherever he will"* (Proverbs 21:1).

Because the Lord is the creator of heaven and earth and has given mankind dominion on earth to glorify their creator, it is appropriate for believers to ask for both earthly and spiritual blessings. It is also important for us to realize that we do not need to be anxious about kings, rulers, dictators, or presidents. God has made it clear in Scripture that every leader is placed to fulfill his plan — always for our good, and sometimes to bring about his judgment and punishment upon men and nations.

Jeremiah wrote to the people in Babylonian captivity:

> *"Build houses and live in them; plant gardens and eat their produce. Take wives and have sons and daughters... seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to the Lord on its behalf, for in its welfare you will find your welfare."* (Jeremiah 29:5–7)

We may not be captives in a foreign land, but as believers we are aliens and strangers in this world. The author of Hebrews said of the patriarchs:

> *"These all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar, and having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth... they desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared for them a city."* (Hebrews 11:13, 16)

Daniel's prayer reminds us that God controls the rise and fall of nations and kings:

> *"Blessed be the name of God forever and ever, to whom belong wisdom and might. He changes times and seasons; he removes kings and sets up kings."* (Daniel 2:20–21)

Nebuchadnezzar himself declared:

> *"His dominion is an everlasting dominion, and his kingdom endures from generation to generation; all the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing, and he does according to his will among the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth; and none can stay his hand or say to him, 'What have you done?'"* (Daniel 4:34–35)

## Yeshua Yahweh: Jesus as Creator and Sustainer

Scripture demonstrates that Jesus — the second person of the Trinity — was the Creator of the world. I may be one of the few who does this, but I sometimes like to refer to Jesus as Yeshua Yahweh, because I think we are too prone to forget that Jesus of the New Testament is the Yahweh of the Old Testament. The Apostle John wrote:

> *"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God. All things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made."* (John 1:1–3)

Paul also affirmed this in 1 Corinthians 8:6:

> *"Yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist."*

And in Colossians 1:16:

> *"For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities — all things were created through him and for him."*

The Scriptures likewise declare that Jesus as creator is also the sustainer of all creation. Colossians 1:17 completes the thought: *"And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together."* Similarly, the author of Hebrews professes:

> *"He is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature, and he upholds the universe by the word of his power."* (Hebrews 1:3)

## Encouragement and Warning

Since Yahweh is the Lord God of heaven and earth — the creator and sustainer of all things, visible and invisible — it is his entitlement to dictate how his creation worships him. The Lord's complaint in Isaiah 65 is against those who forsake his worship, and in Isaiah 66:3–4 against those who claim to worship him but do so in the wrong manner. His affection rests on those who worship him with humility, repentance, and fear. We will continue with that section of the text this afternoon.

The encouragement of this message is to rest in your sovereign Father and your sovereign Savior. He sits enthroned in heaven, everything is within his power and control, he sustains all of his creation, and he holds you in the palm of his hand. Jesus taught his disciples:

> *"My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father's hand."* (John 10:27–29)

Nothing will befall you that God has not intended for your good. Trust him. In your triumph and success, acknowledge the blessings of your covenant-keeping and loving God poured out in your life. In your trouble and despair, find comfort that you are not alone, unseen, or unloved. Even as leaders rise and fall and seasons of time and life change, God's ruling hand controls all these things. As Christianity becomes an increasingly unwelcome term in our society, do everything you can to influence the world around you with a biblical worldview. But of utmost importance, we are called to be faithful to our Lord and to his Word. He is intimately involved in the affairs of men. And if it is time for us to see a decline in the blessings of God in this country because of our rampant sin, then we should pray: come quickly, Lord Jesus.

There is a warning here that must be addressed. If you have not come to God in repentance and faith in Jesus Christ, your life is in jeopardy. Isaiah calls out:

> *"Seek the Lord while he may be found; call upon him while he is near. Let the wicked forsake his way, and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return to the Lord, that he may have compassion on him, and to our God, for he will abundantly pardon."* (Isaiah 55:6–7)

Salvation is found in no one else than Jesus Christ. Turn to him today. As the sovereign creator and owner of all creation, he owns you too — and he tells us how we are to submit to him in obedience. Don't delay. Today is the day of salvation.
